planting.note: Direct sow 4–6 weeks before last frost; harvest baby greens at 35 days and roots at 65–70 days; baby Bull's Blood greens are among the most prized microgreens and salad additions for their stunning color and mild flavor.

directions: Soak multi-seeded beet 'seed balls' in water for 1 hour, then direct sow 1/2 inch deep, thinning to 3–4 inches; Bull's Blood is a historic heirloom (documented in the 1840s) prized primarily for its extraordinarily deep burgundy-maroon foliage that is strikingly ornamental in the garden and border. The young leaves are harvested as microgreens (20–25 days) and baby salad greens (30–40 days) with a mild, earthy-sweet beet flavor; the mature roots are medium-sized with deep red flesh and a good flavor, though many gardeners grow it primarily as a leafy green. An exceptional dual-purpose ornamental and edible beet.

scientific_name: Beta vulgaris subsp. vulgaris var. crassa

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in a deep, loose seedbed; mangel-wurzel produces very large roots (5–20 lbs) requiring deep, stone-free soil to develop properly; widely adapted to poor soils and drought conditions.

directions: Direct sow seed 1/2 inch deep in deeply worked, loose, fertile to moderately fertile soil after last frost; mangel-wurzel is a large-rooted beet developed in Europe for winter livestock feed and human food in times of scarcity, producing enormous roots (up to 20 lbs) with reddish-yellow or white flesh that stores through winter in a root cellar. Young leaves are edible as greens, and the roots (when young, under 3 lbs) can be cooked and eaten like regular beets with a slightly coarser, starchier flavor. Of growing interest to market gardeners, homesteaders, and traditional food preservers for its extraordinary storage qualities and very high yields per square foot.

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost or direct sow again in late summer for fall crop; rainbow chard is biennial but grown as an annual; one of the most ornamental edible garden plants suitable for flower borders as well as vegetable beds.

directions: Direct sow seed 1/2 inch deep in fertile, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil, thinning to 6–12 inches; rainbow chard is a mixture of white, yellow, orange, red, and vivid pink-stemmed chard varieties grown together, producing an exceptional display of color in the garden while providing the same mild, earthy leaves as single-colored chard. The stems of different colors have slightly different flavor intensities (red-stemmed are earthier, white-stemmed milder) but all are equally nutritious and versatile in cooking. Harvest outer leaves regularly to keep plants productive for months. An outstanding cut-and-come-again green for the productive ornamental garden.
